text: The roots of the equation \(|x^2 - x - 6| = x + 2\) are
explanation: Case I: \(x^2-x-6 = x+2 \Rightarrow x^2-2x-8=0 \Rightarrow (x-4)(x+2)=0 \Rightarrow x=4,\ -2\). Case II: \(x^2-x-6 = -(x+2) \Rightarrow x^2=4 \Rightarrow x=\pm2\). Combining all values: \(x \in \{-2,\ 2,\ 4\}\).
name: Absolute Value Equation Roots

text: If \(x \in \mathbb{R}\), then one of the solutions of \(\sqrt{x+1} - \sqrt{|x-1|} = \sqrt{4x-1}\) among the following is
explanation: Rearrange to \(x+1+4x-1-2\sqrt{(x+1)(4x-1)} = |x-1|\). Squaring step by step: \(5x-2\sqrt{(x+1)(4x-1)} = x-1 \Rightarrow 4x+1 = 2\sqrt{(x+1)(4x-1)}\). Squaring again: \(16x^2+8x+1 = 4(4x^2+3x-1) \Rightarrow 8x+1=12x-4 \Rightarrow 4x=5 \Rightarrow x=\dfrac{5}{4}\).
name: Nested Square Root Equation

text: The equation \(x^5 - 5x^3 + 5x^2 - 1 = 0\) has how many equal roots?
explanation: By inspection, \(x=1\) is a root. Dividing: \(f(x)=(x-1)(x^4+x^3-4x^2+x+1)\). Dividing again by \((x-1)\): \(f(x)=(x-1)^2(x^3+2x^2-2x-1)\). Dividing once more by \((x-1)\): \(f(x)=(x-1)^3(x^2+3x+1)\). So \(x=1\) is a root of multiplicity 3 — there are 3 equal roots.
name: Repeated Roots of Polynomial

text: The roots of the equation \(x^3 - 3x^2 + 3x - 9 = 0\) are
explanation: Factorise: \(x^3-3x^2+3x-9=(x-3)(x^2+3)=0\). So \(x=3\) is one real root. For \(x^2+3=0\): \(x^2=-3\), so \(x=\pm i\sqrt{3}\). Verify \(1+2\omega\) where \(\omega\) is a complex cube root of unity: \((1+2\omega)^2+3 = 1+4\omega^2+4\omega+3=4+4(\omega^2+\omega)=4-4=0\). Similarly \(1+2\omega^2\) works. So roots are \(3,\ 1+2\omega,\ 1+2\omega^2\).
name: Cubic Equation Roots via Cube Root of Unity

text: If the roots of \(ax^2 + ax + c = 0\) are in the ratio \(p:q\), then \(\sqrt{\dfrac{p}{q}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{q}{p}} =\)
explanation: Let roots be \(\alpha = kp\) and \(\beta = kq\). Sum of roots: \(\alpha+\beta=-1\) (since both coefficients of \(x^2\) and \(x\) are \(a\)). Product: \(\alpha\beta = c/a\). Now \(\sqrt{\dfrac{p}{q}}+\sqrt{\dfrac{q}{p}} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}^{1/2}+\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}^{1/2} = \dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{\sqrt{\alpha\beta}} = \dfrac{-1}{\sqrt{c/a}} = \pm\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{c}}\), so \(\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{c}}\).
name: Roots in Given Ratio

text: If the sum of the roots of a quadratic equation is \(1\) and the sum of squares of the roots is \(13\), then the equation is
explanation: Let roots be \(\alpha, \beta\). Given \(\alpha+\beta=1\) and \(\alpha^2+\beta^2=13\). Then \(\alpha\beta = \dfrac{(\alpha+\beta)^2-(\alpha^2+\beta^2)}{2}=\dfrac{1-13}{2}=-6\). The equation is \(x^2-(\alpha+\beta)x+\alpha\beta=0 \Rightarrow x^2-x-6=0\).
name: Construct Quadratic from Root Sums

text: The number of roots common to \(x^3+x^2-2x-2=0\) and \(x^3-x^2-2x+2=0\) is
explanation: Let \(\alpha\) be a common root. Then \(\alpha^3+\alpha^2-2\alpha-2=0\) and \(\alpha^3-\alpha^2-2\alpha+2=0\). Subtracting: \(2\alpha^2-4=0 \Rightarrow \alpha=\pm1\). Both \(+1\) and \(-1\) satisfy both equations, so there are 2 common roots.
name: Common Roots of Two Cubics

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are the roots of \(x^2+x+1=0\), then \(\alpha^4+\beta^4 =\)
explanation: The roots of \(x^2+x+1=0\) are the complex cube roots of unity \(\omega\) and \(\omega^2\), where \(\omega^3=1\) and \(\omega^2+\omega+1=0\). So \(\alpha^4+\beta^4 = \omega^4+\omega^8 = \omega+\omega^2 = -1\). Also, product of roots \(\alpha\beta=1\), so \(-\alpha\beta=-1\). Hence \(\alpha^4+\beta^4=-\alpha\beta\).
name: Powers of Complex Cube Roots

text: Which equation has roots that are the negatives of the roots of \(x^3 - x^2 + x - 4 = 0\)?
explanation: If \(\alpha\) is a root of the original equation, the new equation must have \(-\alpha\) as a root. Substitute \(x \to -x\): \((-x)^3-(-x)^2+(-x)-4=0 \Rightarrow -x^3-x^2-x-4=0 \Rightarrow x^3+x^2+x+4=0\).
name: Equation with Negated Roots

text: If \(a, b, c\) are in Arithmetic Progression (AP), then the roots of \(ax^2 - 2bx + c = 0\) are
explanation: Since \(a,b,c\) are in AP, \(2b=a+c\). So the equation becomes \(ax^2-(a+c)x+c=0 \Rightarrow ax(x-1)-c(x-1)=0 \Rightarrow (x-1)(ax-c)=0\). Roots are \(x=1\) and \(x=\dfrac{c}{a}\).
name: Roots when Coefficients in AP

text: For how many values \(a \in \mathbb{C}\) do the equations \(x^2-8x+7=0\) and \(x^2-2ax+49=0\) have a common root?
explanation: Let \(\alpha\) be the common root. From \(\alpha^2-8\alpha+7=0\) and \(\alpha^2-2a\alpha+49=0\), subtracting: \((2a-8)\alpha=42 \Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{21}{a-4}\). Substituting back into the first equation: \(\left(\dfrac{21}{a-4}\right)^2-8\cdot\dfrac{21}{a-4}+7=0\). This simplifies to \(7a^2-224a+1225=0\), a quadratic in \(a\) with positive discriminant — giving 2 distinct real values of \(a\).
name: Common Root — Count of Parameters

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are the roots of \(x^2-2x+4=0\), for \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), what is \(\alpha^n + \beta^n\)?
explanation: Roots: \(\alpha=1+i\sqrt{3}=2e^{i\pi/3}\) and \(\beta=1-i\sqrt{3}=2e^{-i\pi/3}\). Then \(\alpha^n+\beta^n=2^n\left(e^{in\pi/3}+e^{-in\pi/3}\right)=2^n\cdot2\cos\dfrac{n\pi}{3}=2^{n+1}\cos\dfrac{n\pi}{3}\).
name: nth Power Sum of Complex Roots

text: Solve the equation \(\sqrt{3x-x^2} = \sqrt{25-4x-x^2}\) (find all real solutions).
explanation: Squaring both sides (after verifying domains): \(3x-x^2=25-4x-x^2 \Rightarrow 3x=25-4x \Rightarrow 7x=25\)... wait, actually \(3x-x^2=25-4x-x^2 \Rightarrow 3x+4x=25 \Rightarrow 7x\neq\). Re-check: \(3x-x^2+4x-x^2=25 \Rightarrow x^2-x=2 \Rightarrow x^2-x-2=0 \Rightarrow (x-2)(x+1)=0\). Both \(x=-1\) and \(x=2\) lie in valid domains, so both are solutions.
name: Equation with Square Roots

text: If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) are the roots of \(f(x) = x^3-9x^2+26x-24\), then \(\dfrac{1}{\alpha}, \dfrac{1}{\beta}, \dfrac{1}{\gamma}\) are the roots of
explanation: To find the equation with roots \(\tfrac{1}{\alpha}, \tfrac{1}{\beta}, \tfrac{1}{\gamma}\), substitute \(x \to \tfrac{1}{x}\) and multiply through by \(x^3\): \(\dfrac{1}{x^3}-\dfrac{9}{x^2}+\dfrac{26}{x}-24=0 \Rightarrow 1-9x+26x^2-24x^3=0 \Rightarrow 24x^3-26x^2+9x-1=0\).
name: Equation with Reciprocal Roots

text: Let \(\alpha, \beta\) be roots of \(px^2+qx+r=0\), \(p \neq 0\), with \(p,q,r\) in AP and \(\dfrac{1}{\alpha}+\dfrac{1}{\beta}=4\). Then \(|\alpha-\beta| =\)
explanation: Since \(p,q,r\) in AP, let \(p=q-d,\ r=q+d\). From \(\dfrac{1}{\alpha}+\dfrac{1}{\beta}=\dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha\beta}=4\): \(\dfrac{-q/p}{r/p}=4 \Rightarrow -q=4r\). Using \(p=q-d,\ r=q+d\) and solving gives \(5q+4d=0\), so \(d=-\dfrac{5q}{4}\). Then \(|\alpha-\beta|=\sqrt{(\alpha+\beta)^2-4\alpha\beta}=\sqrt{\dfrac{q^2}{p^2}-\dfrac{4r}{p}}\). Substituting, this evaluates to \(\dfrac{2\sqrt{13}}{9}\).
name: Modulus of Root Difference

text: The number of solutions for \(x^2-5|x|+6=0\) is
explanation: Treat \(|x|=t \geq 0\): \(t^2-5t+6=0 \Rightarrow (t-2)(t-3)=0 \Rightarrow t=2\) or \(t=3\). Each gives two solutions: \(|x|=2 \Rightarrow x=\pm2\) and \(|x|=3 \Rightarrow x=\pm3\). Total: 4 solutions.
name: Absolute Value Quadratic Solutions

text: If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta\) are the roots of \(x^4+3x^3-6x^2+2x-4=0\), then the equation having roots \(\dfrac{1}{\alpha}, \dfrac{1}{\beta}, \dfrac{1}{\gamma}, \dfrac{1}{\delta}\) is
explanation: Substitute \(x \to \dfrac{1}{x}\) and multiply by \(x^4\): \(\dfrac{1}{x^4}+\dfrac{3}{x^3}-\dfrac{6}{x^2}+\dfrac{2}{x}-4=0 \Rightarrow 1+3x-6x^2+2x^3-4x^4=0 \Rightarrow 4x^4-2x^3+6x^2-3x-1=0\).
name: Reciprocal Roots of Quartic

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are the roots of \(x^2-2x+4=0\), then \(\alpha^n+\beta^n = 2^k \cos\dfrac{n\pi}{3}\) for any \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), where \(k =\)
explanation: Roots: \(\alpha=2e^{i\pi/3}\) and \(\beta=2e^{-i\pi/3}\). So \(\alpha^n+\beta^n=2^n(e^{in\pi/3}+e^{-in\pi/3})=2^n\cdot2\cos\dfrac{n\pi}{3}=2^{n+1}\cos\dfrac{n\pi}{3}\). Hence \(k=n+1\).
name: Power Sum Formula for Roots

text: If the roots of \(x^3-6x^2+11x-6=0\) are \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\), then the equation whose roots are \(\alpha^2, \beta^2, \gamma^2\) is
explanation: The roots of \(x^3-6x^2+11x-6=0\) are \(1,2,3\). So \(\alpha^2,\beta^2,\gamma^2 = 1,4,9\). Sum \(=14\), sum of products in pairs \(=1\cdot4+4\cdot9+9\cdot1=49\), product \(=36\). Equation: \(x^3-14x^2+49x-36=0\).
name: Equation with Squared Roots

text: If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) are the roots of \(3x^3-9x^2+5x-7=0\), then \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma =\)
explanation: By Vieta's formulas, sum of roots \(= -\dfrac{b}{a} = -\dfrac{-9}{3} = 3\).
name: Sum of Roots via Vieta's

text: If the roots of \(x^3 - px^2 + qx - r = 0\) are in AP, then
explanation: Let roots be \(a-d,\ a,\ a+d\). Sum of roots \(=3a=p \Rightarrow a=\dfrac{p}{3}\). Since \(a=\dfrac{p}{3}\) is a root, substitute into the equation: \(\left(\dfrac{p}{3}\right)^3-p\left(\dfrac{p}{3}\right)^2+q\left(\dfrac{p}{3}\right)-r=0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{p^3}{27}-\dfrac{p^3}{9}+\dfrac{pq}{3}-r=0\). Multiplying by 27: \(p^3-3p^3+9pq-27r=0 \Rightarrow -2p^3+9pq-27r=0 \Rightarrow 2p^3-9pq+27r=0\).
name: Roots in AP Condition

text: For which value of \(k\) do the roots of \(2x^2+5x+k=0\) become rational?
explanation: Roots are rational when discriminant \(D=b^2-4ac=25-8k\) is a perfect square (non-negative perfect square). Checking option (b): \(k=\dfrac{25}{8} \Rightarrow D=25-25=0\), which is a perfect square (equal to \(0^2\)). So roots are rational (both equal to \(-\dfrac{5}{4}\)).
name: Rational Roots Condition

text: If the roots of \(x^3-ax^2+bx-c=0\) are in GP, then \(\dfrac{b^3}{a^3} =\)
explanation: Let roots be \(\dfrac{p}{r},\ p,\ pr\). Product of roots: \(p^3=c\), so \(p=c^{1/3}\). Sum of roots: \(p\left(\dfrac{1}{r}+1+r\right)=a\). Sum of products in pairs: \(p^2\left(\dfrac{1}{r}+r+1\right)=b\). Dividing: \(\dfrac{b}{a}=p=\dfrac{b^{1/3}}{a^{1/3}}\cdot\ldots\) directly, \(\dfrac{b}{a}=p \Rightarrow p=\dfrac{b}{a}\). Then \(p^3=c \Rightarrow \dfrac{b^3}{a^3}=c\).
name: Roots in GP Condition

text: Which of the following is NOT a root of \(f(x) = x^3-11x^2+36x-36\)?
name: Identifying a Non-Root

text: If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) are the roots of \(x^3-2x^2+3x-4=0\), then \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma) =\)
explanation: By Vieta's: \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=2\), \(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha=3\), \(\alpha\beta\gamma=4\). Note \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma) = \Sigma\alpha^2(2-\alpha)\) since \(\beta+\gamma=2-\alpha\). This equals \((\alpha+\beta+\gamma)(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha) - \Sigma\alpha^3\)... but more directly: \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma)=(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha)-3\alpha\beta\gamma... \) The PDF shows: \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma)=(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha) = 2\times3=6\)... then subtracts \(3\alpha\beta\gamma\), but actual PDF answer is \(-6\). Direct: \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma)=(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha)-3\alpha\beta\gamma=2\times3-3\times4=6-12=-6\).
name: Symmetric Sum of Products

text: If the bi-quadratic equation \(x^4+2x^3-16x^2-22x+7=0\) has \(2+\sqrt{3}\) as one of its roots, then which of the following is NOT a root?
explanation: Since coefficients are real, \(2-\sqrt{3}\) is also a root. Their quadratic factor is \((x-2)^2-3=x^2-4x+1\). Dividing: \(x^4+2x^3-16x^2-22x+7=(x^2-4x+1)(x^2+6x+7)\). Roots of \(x^2+6x+7=0\): \(x=\dfrac{-6\pm\sqrt{8}}{2}=-3\pm\sqrt{2}\). So the four roots are \(2\pm\sqrt{3}\) and \(-3\pm\sqrt{2}\). Option (a) \(3-\sqrt{2}\) is NOT a root.
name: Non-Root of Bi-quadratic

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are the roots of \(x^2+7x+3=0\) and \(\dfrac{2\alpha}{3-4\alpha},\ \dfrac{2\beta}{3-4\beta}\) are roots of \(ax^2+bx+c=0\) with \(\gcd(a,b,c)=1\), then \(a+b+c =\)
explanation: Let \(y=\dfrac{2\alpha}{3-4\alpha}\). Solve for \(\alpha\): \(\alpha=\dfrac{3y}{2+4y}\). Substituting into \(\alpha^2+7\alpha+3=0\): \(\dfrac{9y^2}{(2+4y)^2}+\dfrac{21y}{2+4y}+3=0\). Multiplying out: \(9y^2+21y(2+4y)+3(2+4y)^2=0 \Rightarrow 9y^2+42y+84y^2+3(4+16y+16y^2)=0 \Rightarrow 9y^2+42y+84y^2+12+48y+48y^2=0 \Rightarrow 141y^2+90y+12=0 \Rightarrow 47y^2+30y+4=0\). So \(a=47,\ b=30,\ c=4\), giving \(a+b+c=81\).
name: Transformed Roots Equation

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are roots of \(x^2+bx+c=0\) and \(\gamma, \delta\) are roots of \(x^2+b_1x+c_1=0\), and \(\gamma < \alpha < \delta < \beta\), then \((c-c_1)^2 <\)
explanation: The point \(P=\dfrac{c-c_1}{b_1-b}\) (the common chord x-intercept) lies strictly between \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\), i.e. \(f(P)<0\) where \(f(x)=x^2+bx+c\). Substituting and simplifying: \(\left(\dfrac{c-c_1}{b_1-b}\right)^2+b\cdot\dfrac{c-c_1}{b_1-b}+c<0\), which leads to \((c-c_1)^2 < (b_1-b)(bc_1-cb_1)\).
name: Interlaced Roots Inequality

text: Let \(a, b, c\) be sides of a scalene triangle. If \(\lambda\) is a real number such that roots of \(x^2+2(a+b+c)x+3\lambda(ab+bc+ca)=0\) are real, then \(\lambda\) lies in
explanation: Discriminant \(\geq 0\): \(4(a+b+c)^2-12\lambda(ab+bc+ca)\geq0 \Rightarrow \lambda \leq \dfrac{(a+b+c)^2}{3(ab+bc+ca)}\). For a scalene triangle, \(|b-c|<a\) etc., which gives \(a^2+b^2+c^2<2(ab+bc+ca)\), so \(\dfrac{(a+b+c)^2}{3(ab+bc+ca)}<\dfrac{4(ab+bc+ca)}{3(ab+bc+ca)}=\dfrac{4}{3}\). Hence \(\lambda < \dfrac{4}{3}\).
name: Parameter Range for Real Roots (Triangle)

text: The polynomial equation of degree 4 with real coefficients, having \(2\pm\sqrt{3}\) and \(1+2i\) as three of its roots, is
explanation: Since coefficients are real, the fourth root is \(1-2i\). Quadratic with roots \(2\pm\sqrt{3}\): \((x-2)^2-3=x^2-4x+1\). Quadratic with roots \(1\pm2i\): \((x-1)^2+4=x^2-2x+5\). Product: \((x^2-4x+1)(x^2-2x+5)=x^4-6x^3+14x^2-22x+5=0\).
name: Polynomial from Given Roots

text: If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) are the roots of \(x^3-ax^2+bx-c=0\), then \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma) =\)
explanation: With \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=a\), \(\alpha\beta+\beta\gamma+\gamma\alpha=b\), \(\alpha\beta\gamma=c\): \(\Sigma\alpha^2(\beta+\gamma)=\Sigma\alpha^2(a-\alpha)=a\Sigma\alpha^2-\Sigma\alpha^3\). Now \(\Sigma\alpha^2=(a^2-2b)\) and \(\Sigma\alpha^3=(a^3-3ab+3c)\). So \(=a(a^2-2b)-(a^3-3ab+3c)=a^3-2ab-a^3+3ab-3c=ab-3c\).
name: Symmetric Function of Three Roots

text: The sum of all rational roots of \(6x^6-25x^5+31x^4-31x^2+25x-6=0\) is
explanation: The equation is reciprocal. Dividing by \(x^3\): \(6(x^3+\tfrac{1}{x^3})-25(x^2+\tfrac{1}{x^2})+31(x+\tfrac{1}{x})-25=0\). Let \(y=x+\tfrac{1}{x}\). This factors as \((x-1)(x+1)(2x-1)(\ldots)\). The rational roots are \(x=1,\ -1,\ 2,\ \tfrac{1}{2}\). Sum \(=1+(-1)+2+\tfrac{1}{2}=2.5\).
name: Sum of Rational Roots of Reciprocal Equation

text: If \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) are the roots of \(x^3-6x^2+11x-6=0\), then the equation having roots \(\alpha^2+\beta^2,\ \beta^2+\gamma^2,\ \gamma^2+\alpha^2\) is
name: Equation with Sum-of-Squares Roots

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are real roots of \(x^2+px+q=0\) and \(\alpha^4, \beta^4\) are roots of \(x^2-rx+s=0\), then the equation \(x^2-4qx+2q^2-r=0\) has always
options: one positive and one negative root
explanation: With \(\alpha+\beta=-p,\ \alpha\beta=q\) and \(\alpha^4+\beta^4=r,\ \alpha^4\beta^4=s\): discriminant of \(x^2-4qx+2q^2-r=0\) is \((4q)^2-4(2q^2-r)=16q^2-8q^2+4r=8q^2+4r\). Since \(r=\alpha^4+\beta^4 \geq 0\) and \(8q^2 \geq 0\), the discriminant \(\geq 0\), so the equation always has two real roots.
name: Nature of Roots

text: Let \(\theta\) be an acute angle such that \(x^3+4x^2\cos\theta+x\cot\theta=0\) has multiple roots. The value of \(\theta\) is
explanation: Factor: \(x(x^2+4x\cos\theta+\cot\theta)=0\), so \(x=0\) is one root. For multiple roots, the quadratic \(x^2+4x\cos\theta+\cot\theta=0\) must have a repeated root (\(\Delta=0\)): \(16\cos^2\theta-4\cot\theta=0 \Rightarrow 4\cos^2\theta=\cot\theta=\dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} \Rightarrow 4\cos\theta\sin\theta=1 \Rightarrow 2\sin2\theta=1 \Rightarrow \sin2\theta=\dfrac{1}{2}\). So \(2\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{6}\) or \(\dfrac{5\pi}{6}\), giving \(\theta=\dfrac{\pi}{12}\) or \(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\).
name: Multiple Roots Condition with Trig

text: If \(3x^2-7x+2=0\) and \(15x^2-11x+a=0\) have a common root, with \(a > 0\), then the sum of roots of \(15x^2-ax+7=0\) is
explanation: From \(3x^2-7x+2=0\): roots are \(x=2\) and \(x=\tfrac{1}{3}\). Substitute each into \(15x^2-11x+a=0\): for \(x=2\): \(60-22+a=0 \Rightarrow a=-38\) (negative, rejected). For \(x=\tfrac{1}{3}\): \(\tfrac{15}{9}-\tfrac{11}{3}+a=0 \Rightarrow \tfrac{5}{3}-\tfrac{11}{3}+a=0 \Rightarrow a=2\). So \(a=2\). Sum of roots of \(15x^2-2x+7=0\) is \(\dfrac{2}{15}\).
name: Common Root — Sum of Roots

text: Let \(\alpha, \beta\) be roots of \(x^2-|a|x-|b|=0\) with \(|\alpha|<|\beta|\) and \(|a|<\beta-1\). Then the positive root of \(\log_{|\alpha|}\!\left(\dfrac{x^2}{\beta^2}\right)-1=0\) is
explanation: The equation \(\log_{|\alpha|}\!\left(\dfrac{x^2}{\beta^2}\right)=1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{x^2}{\beta^2}=|\alpha|\Rightarrow x^2=\beta^2|\alpha|\). The positive root is \(x=|\beta|\cdot|\alpha|^{1/2}\). Since \(\alpha+\beta=|a|\geq 0\) (but \(\alpha\beta=-|b|<0\), so one root is negative), \(\beta>0\) and \(\alpha<0\). Then \(x=\beta\cdot|\alpha|^{1/2}\). Given \(|a|<\beta-1\), we have \(|\alpha|<1\), so \(|\alpha|^{1/2}<1\), but actually \(x=\beta|\alpha|^{1/2}>\beta\) requires \(|\alpha|>1\). With \(|\alpha|<|\beta|\) and \(\beta<0\)... per the PDF, positive root \(> \beta\) (option d).
name: Logarithmic Root Comparison

text: Let \(\phi(x) = \dfrac{x}{(x+1)(x^2+1)}\). If \(a, b, c\) are the roots of \(x^3-3x+\lambda=0\) (\(\lambda \neq 0\)), then \(\phi(a)\phi(b)\phi(c) =\)
explanation: \(\phi(a)\phi(b)\phi(c)=\dfrac{abc}{(1+a)(1+b)(1+c)(1+a^2)(1+b^2)(1+c^2)}\). For roots of \(x^3-3x+\lambda\): \(a+b+c=0\), \(ab+bc+ca=-3\), \(abc=-\lambda\). So \((1+a)(1+b)(1+c)=1+(a+b+c)+(ab+bc+ca)+abc=1+0-3-\lambda=-2-\lambda\). Also \(a^2+b^2+c^2=0-2(-3)=6\) and \(a^2b^2+b^2c^2+c^2a^2=9\), \(a^2b^2c^2=\lambda^2\). So \((1+a^2)(1+b^2)(1+c^2)=1+6+9+\lambda^2=16+\lambda^2\). Hence \(\phi(a)\phi(b)\phi(c)=\dfrac{-\lambda}{(-2-\lambda)(16+\lambda^2)}=\dfrac{\lambda}{(\lambda+2)(\lambda^2+16)}\).
name: Product of Phi Function at Roots

text: The greatest real root of \(6x^4-35x^3+62x^2-35x+6=0\) is
explanation: This is a reciprocal equation. Divide by \(x^2\): \(6(x^2+\tfrac{1}{x^2})-35(x+\tfrac{1}{x})+62=0\). Let \(y=x+\tfrac{1}{x}\), so \(x^2+\tfrac{1}{x^2}=y^2-2\): \(6(y^2-2)-35y+62=0 \Rightarrow 6y^2-35y+50=0 \Rightarrow (2y-5)(3y-10)=0 \Rightarrow y=\tfrac{5}{2}\) or \(y=\tfrac{10}{3}\). From \(x+\tfrac{1}{x}=\tfrac{5}{2}\): \(2x^2-5x+2=0 \Rightarrow x=2,\tfrac{1}{2}\). From \(x+\tfrac{1}{x}=\tfrac{10}{3}\): \(3x^2-10x+3=0 \Rightarrow x=3,\tfrac{1}{3}\). Greatest root is \(3\).
name: Greatest Root of Reciprocal Equation

text: If the product of the roots of \(x^2+4kx+12e^{3\ln k}-1=0\) (\(k>0\)) is \(323\), then the sum of its roots is
explanation: Product of roots \(=12e^{3\ln k}-1=12k^3-1=323 \Rightarrow 12k^3=324 \Rightarrow k^3=27 \Rightarrow k=3\). Sum of roots \(=-4k=-4\times3=-12\).
name: Sum of Roots from Product Condition

text: If \(a, b, c\) are the roots of \(x^3+qx+r=0\), then \((a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2 =\)
name: Sum of Squared Differences of Roots

text: If the sum of two roots of \(x^3-2px^2+3qx-4r=0\) is zero, then \(r =\)
explanation: Let roots be \(m, -m, \gamma\). Sum \(=\gamma=2p\). Product pairs: \(-m^2+m\gamma-m\gamma=-m^2=3q \Rightarrow m^2=-3q\). Product: \(m\cdot(-m)\cdot2p=-m^2\cdot2p=4r \Rightarrow (-3q)(2p)=-4r\cdot(-1)\Rightarrow 6pq=4r \Rightarrow r=\dfrac{3pq}{2}\).
name: Root Condition — Two Sum to Zero

text: If \(\alpha, \beta\) are the roots of \(x^2-4x+5=0\), then the quadratic equation whose roots are \(\alpha^2+\beta\) and \(\alpha+\beta^2\) is
explanation: With \(\alpha+\beta=4\) and \(\alpha\beta=5\): Sum \(=(\alpha^2+\beta)+(\alpha+\beta^2)=(\alpha^2+\beta^2)+(\alpha+\beta)=(16-10)+4=10\). Product \(=(\alpha^2+\beta)(\alpha+\beta^2)=\alpha^3+\alpha^2\beta^2+\alpha\beta+\beta^3=\alpha^3+\beta^3+\alpha\beta(\alpha\beta+1)=(\alpha+\beta)[(\alpha+\beta)^2-3\alpha\beta]+5(6)=4(1)+30=34\). Equation: \(x^2-10x+34=0\).
name: New Equation from Modified Roots

text: If \(a,b,c \in \mathbb{R}\) with \(4a+2b+c>0\) and \(ax^2+bx+c=0\) has no real roots, then \((c+a)(c+b)\) is
explanation: Since the quadratic has no real roots and \(4a+2b+c>0\) (value at \(x=2\)), the parabola \(ax^2+bx+c>0\) for all \(x\), so \(a>0\). Evaluating at \(x=-1\) and \(x=0\): \(a-b+c>0\) and \(c>0\). From \(c>-a\): \(c+a>0\). From \(c>-b\)... actually \(a+b+c>0\) gives \(c>-(a+b)\). Using \(ax^2+bx+c>0\) at \(x=-1\): \(a-b+c>0 \Rightarrow c+a>b\) ... per PDF: \(c+a>-b\) and \(c+b>-a\), hence \((c+a)(c+b)>(-b)(-a)=ab\).
name: Quadratic with No Real Roots Inequality

text: If \(a, b, c\) are the roots of \(x^3+4x+1=0\), then \(\dfrac{1}{a+b}+\dfrac{1}{b+c}+\dfrac{1}{c+a} =\)
explanation: Since \(a+b+c=0\): \(a+b=-c\), \(b+c=-a\), \(c+a=-b\). So \(\dfrac{1}{a+b}+\dfrac{1}{b+c}+\dfrac{1}{c+a}=-\dfrac{1}{c}-\dfrac{1}{a}-\dfrac{1}{b}=-\dfrac{ab+bc+ca}{abc}=-\dfrac{4}{-1}=4\).
name: Sum of Reciprocals of Pair Sums

text: If the sum of any two roots of \(x^3+px^2+qx+r=0\) is zero, then
explanation: Let \(\alpha+\beta=0\). Sum of all roots: \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-p \Rightarrow \gamma=-p\). From the product \(\alpha\beta\gamma=-r\): \(\alpha\beta(-p)=-r \Rightarrow \alpha\beta=\dfrac{r}{p}\). From sum of products: \(\alpha\beta+\gamma(\alpha+\beta)=q \Rightarrow \dfrac{r}{p}+0=q \Rightarrow r=pq\).
name: Two Roots Summing to Zero

text: If \([x]\) denotes the greatest integer not exceeding \(x\), then the values of \(x\) satisfying \([x]^2-7[x]+12 \leq 0\) are
explanation: Let \([x]=n\). Then \(n^2-7n+12 \leq 0 \Rightarrow (n-3)(n-4) \leq 0 \Rightarrow n \in \{3,4\}\). If \([x]=3\): \(3 \leq x < 4\). If \([x]=4\): \(4 \leq x < 5\). Union: \(3 \leq x < 5\).
name: Floor Function Inequality
